name: unified_ui_phase2_seamless
outcome_goal: Wire builder save/load, trigger setup, chat panel, and mode transitions into the unified shell

jobs.label: builder_save_and_load
jobs.prompt:
## Objective
Add Save/Load workflow functionality to the builder so workflows persist to the dashboard.

## Working Directory
`Code&DBs/Workflow/surfaces/app/src/builder/`

## Context
The builder is at `BuilderPage.tsx`. It currently has a pipeline state (`PipelineModel | null`) and dispatches via `useDispatch`. It needs to:
1. Accept a `workflowId` prop to load an existing workflow
2. Have a "Save" button that writes to `POST /api/workflows`
3. Have a "Back" button that returns to dashboard mode
4. After saving, the workflow appears on the dashboard

The `workflows` table has: id, name, description, definition (JSONB), compiled_spec (JSONB), tags, version, is_template.
The `pipelineToQueueSpec()` function in `queueSpecExport.ts` converts a PipelineModel to a QueueSpec.

## Task 1: Add props to BuilderPage

Read `BuilderPage.tsx` first. Add optional props:
```typescript
interface BuilderPageProps {
  workflowId?: string | null;  // load existing workflow
  onBack?: () => void;         // return to dashboard
}
```

When `workflowId` is provided:
- On mount, fetch `GET /api/workflows/{workflowId}` 
- Parse the `definition` field as a PipelineModel and set it as the pipeline state
- Set the intent from the workflow name
- Show the workflow name in the nav bar

## Task 2: Add Save button

Add a "Save" button to the toolbar (next to Export JSON and Run):
```tsx
<button className="builder-btn" onClick={handleSave} disabled={!hasSteps}>
  {workflowId ? 'Save' : 'Save As...'}
</button>
```

`handleSave` function:
1. Build the compiled_spec via `pipelineToQueueSpec(pipeline)`
2. POST to `/api/workflows` with body:
   ```json
   {
     "id": workflowId || undefined,
     "name": pipeline.name || intent || 'Untitled Workflow',
     "description": pipeline.goal || '',
     "definition": pipeline,
     "compiled_spec": compiledSpec
   }
   ```
3. On success, set workflowId from response
4. Show a brief "Saved" indicator (set a state variable, clear after 2s)

## Task 3: Add Back button

In the nav bar (the top bar with Helm + Builder), add a back arrow before "Helm":
```tsx
{onBack && (
  <button onClick={onBack} style={{...}}>
    ← Dashboard
  </button>
)}
```

## Task 4: Add saved indicator

When save succeeds, show a small green "Saved ✓" text next to the Save button for 2 seconds.

## IMPORTANT
- Read BuilderPage.tsx fully before modifying
- The existing toolbar has: + Agent, + API, + Check, + Human | spacer | metrics | Export JSON, Run
- Add Save BEFORE Export JSON
- Don't break existing functionality
- Use `fetch('/api/workflows', { method: 'POST', ... })` for saving
- The API expects JSON body with Content-Type application/json

jobs.label: trigger_setup_panel
jobs.prompt:
## Objective
Create an inline trigger configuration panel for the builder.

## Working Directory
`Code&DBs/Workflow/surfaces/app/src/builder/`

## Context
After a workflow is saved, the user should be able to set up a trigger (when should this workflow auto-run). The `workflow_triggers` table has: id, workflow_id, event_type, filter (JSONB), enabled, cron_expression.

API: `POST /api/workflow-triggers` to create/update triggers.

## Create: `builder/TriggerSetup.tsx`

Props:
```typescript
interface TriggerSetupProps {
  workflowId: string;
  onClose: () => void;
}
```

The component fetches existing trigger for this workflow on mount, then allows editing.

Layout:
```
┌─ Set Trigger ──────────────────────── [×] ─┐
│                                              │
│  When should this workflow run?              │
│                                              │
│  Event Type: [dropdown]                      │
│    • When a run succeeds                    │
│    • When a run fails                       │
│    • On a schedule                          │
│    • When a bug is filed                    │
│    • Manual only                            │
│                                              │
│  [conditional fields based on event type]   │
│                                              │
│  When "run succeeds/fails":                 │
│    Filter by spec name: [input]             │
│                                              │
│  When "schedule":                           │
│    Cron expression: [input]                 │
│    (e.g., 0 23 * * * = daily at 11 PM)     │
│                                              │
│  [Enable/Disable toggle]                    │
│                                              │
│  [Save Trigger]  [Remove Trigger]           │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Event type mapping to system_events:
- "When a run succeeds" → event_type: 'run.succeeded'
- "When a run fails" → event_type: 'run.failed'
- "On a schedule" → event_type: 'schedule.fired'
- "When a bug is filed" → event_type: 'db.bugs.insert'
- "Manual only" → no trigger created

Save: POST to `/api/workflow-triggers` with `{workflow_id, event_type, filter, cron_expression, enabled}`

CSS: Use the builder's existing CSS classes: builder-section, builder-section__label, builder-input, builder-select, builder-btn, builder-btn--primary, builder-btn--accent.

Additional styles:
```css
.trigger-setup { padding: 16px; background: var(--bg-card); border: 1px solid var(--border); border-radius: 10px; margin: 12px 20px; }
.trigger-setup__header { display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; margin-bottom: 16px; }
.trigger-setup__title { font-size: 15px; font-weight: 600; }
.trigger-setup__close { background: none; border: none; color: var(--text-muted); cursor: pointer; font-size: 16px; }
.trigger-setup__toggle { display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; margin: 12px 0; }
```

Append these to builder.css.

## IMPORTANT
- Read the builder.css file first to understand existing patterns
- Use builder-section, builder-section__label, builder-input, builder-select classes
- The component should be self-contained (fetches its own data)
- Generate trigger IDs as 'trg_' + random hex
- Export as named export

jobs.label: chat_panel
jobs.prompt:
## Objective
Convert the chat interface from a full page into a sliding panel that overlays the right side of the dashboard.

## Working Directory
`Code&DBs/Workflow/surfaces/app/src/`

## Context
The current chat lives in `workspace/WorkspacePage.tsx` as a full page (HelmPage). It uses:
- `useChat()` hook from `workspace/useChat.ts` for conversation state
- `ChatMessage` component from `workspace/ChatMessage.tsx` for rendering messages
- `MarkdownRenderer` from `workspace/MarkdownRenderer.tsx`
- `ToolResultRenderer` from `workspace/ToolResultRenderer.tsx`

The chat needs to become a panel that slides in from the right when activated, without replacing the dashboard content underneath.

## Create: `dashboard/ChatPanel.tsx`

A sliding panel component that wraps the chat functionality.

Props:
```typescript
interface ChatPanelProps {
  open: boolean;
  onClose: () => void;
}
```

Layout:
```
┌─ Chat Panel (400px, slides from right) ────┐
│  ┌─ Header ────────────────────── [×] ─┐   │
│  │  Ask Anything                        │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│                                             │
│  [message feed - scrollable]               │
│                                             │
│  ┌─ Input ─────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │  Type a message...          [Send]  │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────┘   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Implementation:
1. Import `useChat` from `../workspace/useChat`
2. Use the same message rendering as WorkspacePage but in a narrow panel
3. Animate: when `open` is true, panel slides in from right (transform: translateX(0)); when false, slides out (translateX(100%))
4. Overlay: semi-transparent backdrop that closes panel on click
5. Auto-focus the input when panel opens
6. Auto-scroll to latest message

CSS (add to dashboard.css or create chat-panel.css):
```css
.chat-panel-backdrop { position: fixed; inset: 0; background: rgba(0,0,0,0.3); z-index: 100; opacity: 0; transition: opacity 0.2s; pointer-events: none; }
.chat-panel-backdrop--open { opacity: 1; pointer-events: auto; }
.chat-panel { position: fixed; top: 0; right: 0; bottom: 0; width: 420px; background: var(--bg); border-left: 1px solid var(--border); z-index: 101; display: flex; flex-direction: column; transform: translateX(100%); transition: transform 0.25s ease; }
.chat-panel--open { transform: translateX(0); }
.chat-panel__header { padding: 16px 20px; border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border); display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; }
.chat-panel__title { font-size: 15px; font-weight: 600; }
.chat-panel__close { background: none; border: none; color: var(--text-muted); cursor: pointer; font-size: 18px; padding: 4px; }
.chat-panel__close:hover { color: var(--text); }
.chat-panel__messages { flex: 1; overflow-y: auto; padding: 16px 20px; display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 12px; }
.chat-panel__input-bar { padding: 12px 16px; border-top: 1px solid var(--border); display: flex; gap: 8px; }
.chat-panel__input { flex: 1; padding: 10px 14px; border-radius: 8px; border: 1px solid var(--border); background: var(--bg-card); color: var(--text); font-size: 13px; resize: none; }
.chat-panel__input:focus { outline: none; border-color: var(--accent); }
.chat-panel__send { padding: 8px 16px; border-radius: 8px; border: none; background: var(--accent); color: #000; font-weight: 600; font-size: 13px; cursor: pointer; }
.chat-panel__send:disabled { opacity: 0.5; cursor: not-allowed; }
```

Message rendering: For each message, render:
- User messages: right-aligned, with background var(--bg-card)
- Assistant messages: left-aligned, using MarkdownRenderer
- Tool results: using ToolResultRenderer (imported from workspace/)

The useChat hook handles all the API interaction. Just call `sendMessage(input)` on submit.

## IMPORTANT
- Read workspace/useChat.ts first to understand the hook interface
- Read workspace/ChatMessage.tsx to understand message rendering
- The panel should work independently of the dashboard content
- Keep it simple — basic chat, no sidebar, no selection bar
- Export as named export
- CSS goes in a new file `dashboard/chat-panel.css` imported by the component

jobs.label: wire_modes
jobs.prompt:
## Objective
Wire all the new components into App.tsx so mode transitions work seamlessly.

## Working Directory
`Code&DBs/Workflow/surfaces/app/src/`

## Context
Read `App.tsx` first. It has a mode state machine with modes: dashboard, build, run-detail, chat.

The following components now exist:
- `dashboard/Dashboard.tsx` — landing page with workflow cards (already wired)
- `dashboard/RunDetailView.tsx` — run results view (already wired)
- `dashboard/ChatPanel.tsx` — sliding chat panel (NEW, needs wiring)
- `builder/BuilderPage.tsx` — now accepts `workflowId` and `onBack` props (MODIFIED)
- `builder/TriggerSetup.tsx` — trigger config panel (NEW, used inside builder)

## Task 1: Wire ChatPanel

The chat should be a panel overlay, not a full page replacement. Change the App.tsx so:
- Chat mode renders the ChatPanel ON TOP of whatever mode is underneath (dashboard or build)
- Add a `chatOpen` boolean state separate from mode
- When "Ask anything" is clicked in dashboard, set chatOpen=true (don't change mode)
- When chat panel is closed, set chatOpen=false

```typescript
const [chatOpen, setChatOpen] = useState(false);
```

Render ChatPanel at the bottom of AppShell, OUTSIDE the mode switch:
```tsx
<ChatPanel open={chatOpen} onClose={() => setChatOpen(false)} />
```

Update Dashboard's `onChat` to `() => setChatOpen(true)` instead of `navigate('chat')`.

Remove the `state.mode === 'chat'` case that renders HelmPage — chat is now always a panel overlay.

## Task 2: Wire BuilderPage with props

The builder mode should pass workflowId and onBack:
```tsx
{state.mode === 'build' && (
  <BuilderPage
    workflowId={state.workflowId}
    onBack={() => navigate('dashboard')}
  />
)}
```

## Task 3: Add keyboard shortcut

Cmd+K should toggle chatOpen. Escape should close chat if open, otherwise go to dashboard.

## Task 4: Import new components

Add imports for ChatPanel. Use React.lazy for ChatPanel if it doesn't exist yet:
```tsx
const ChatPanel = React.lazy(() =>
  import('./dashboard/ChatPanel').then(m => ({ default: m.ChatPanel })).catch(() => ({
    default: () => null
  }))
);
```

## IMPORTANT
- Read App.tsx fully before modifying
- Keep the error boundary
- Keep the React.Suspense wrapper
- Don't break the dashboard or builder modes
- The ChatPanel renders as a fixed-position overlay — it doesn't affect layout
- Test: dashboard visible underneath when chat panel is open
